replies.text:
Define "foreign" versus "American".  Movies are frequently made across multiple countries.  It's not just the principal filming in a studio.  There's location filming, there's editing, there's soundtrack composition and recording, there's design work, pre-vis and a thousand and one other tasks that go into making a movie.  There are visual and audio effects which usually involve multiple companies, especially on big blockbusters because it's too much work for any one organisation to do within the required timeframe.

How do you calculate and apply tariffs on all that?

author: @Typecast-L
text:
This is one of the few times I MASSIVLY disagree with tariffs or the reasoning behind them. Hollywood's decline isn't due to foreign competition, it's because it's been churning out uninspired, agenda-driven content focused on Woke, DEI, and ESG narratives. Blaming international productions misses the mark entirely.

Other countries producing movies isn’t an attack on America, and even with Hollywood's recent failures, it's still considered the gold standard, largely because no one else is doing much better. But let’s be honest, you can’t just make every movie in America. 

Foreign films already struggle to gain traction in the US. This tariff will hurt American studios, workers, and creative projects more than it helps. It’s not like manufacturing, where production can relocate and prices can normalize over time. Film doesn’t always work like that. If a story is set in Normandy, it helps to actually film at least parts of it in Normandy. Tariffing international productions is essentially tariffing ourselves. 

And calling this a national security threat? That’s absurd. There's no wave of anti-American propaganda sweeping the world through cinema. In fact, for years, it’s the rest of the world that has had to stomach American political messaging through Hollywood. If anything, America owes the world an apology on that front.
